Here's an interesting question regarding the use of the authority principle. Suppose you have a meeting scheduled with potential customers or clients on a topic about which you are truly knowledgeable. How should you convey your credentials and experience before making your pitch at the meeting? Well, there's something you absolutely shouldn't do, and that is try to present those authority credentials yourself, at least not in person. If you do, your audience may well see you as arrogant and unlikeable. People don't like or trust braggarts. In fact, the newest research shows that even highly competent individuals who try to self-promote in a meeting lose the persuasive advantage their competence gives them by becoming seen as unpleasant and untrustworthy. So what can you do instead? I'd suggest either of two options. First, you can arrange for someone at the meeting, especially someone who knows of your expertise, to introduce you along with your genuine credentials. That way, your audience gets properly informed of your authority status in the matter at hand, but you don't have to look like a boastful egotist in the process. What if you are in a situation in which there is no one who can introduce you? I'd recommend sending evidence of your experience and successes to your audience ahead of your personal appearance in front of them. Your message could say something like, I'm looking forward to our meeting next Thursday on the topic of X. I've attached a copy of my resume or a link to my LinkedIn profile where you can see my background and experience on the topic. So in this way, you can honestly present information about your qualifications as long as you don't do it in person, where the norms of courteous social interaction frown on it. Now, let's take this insight about how and when the principle of authority works best one step further. Wise leaders leverage the principle of authority with their teams by creating systems that consistently endorse or credentialize the members of their teams for all to see so that the team members don't have to do it themselves. Let's take an example from a situation in which a customer or client is handed off from one person to another inside an organization. It's one of the most bungled opportunities for influence in all of business. When we are introduced to an organization by one person and then get handed off to someone else, we can often feel demoted in that relationship if it is handled poorly or if the handoff is to a more junior person. There are many business situations where a contract is signed with a salesperson and then the work transitions to other team members. It's crucial to handle these handoffs well by making sure the customer or client is informed about the credentials of new team members who, remember, can't effectively do it themselves. A related example is illustrated by the experience of a London real estate agency that was having trouble converting potential clients from mere callers to actual clients. For help, the agency contacted my colleague in London, Steve J. Martin, who noticed something telling as he toured the agency. When the receptionist took a call in, she'd ask, are you interested in residential or commercial real estate? Then she'd ask, what area of London are you looking at? With this information collected, she'd say, hold on, I'm going to transfer you to our agent Michelle or Clive or Kim. Steve suggested a small but crucial change in what the receptionist should say, such as, hold on, I'm going to transfer you to Michelle, who has over 30 years of experience in commercial real estate in that part of London. Or I'm going to transfer you to Clive, who knows more about residential real estate there than anyone in the agency. Once this small change was in place, the firm experienced a 16% increase in signed contracts. And here's the best thing, it was all true. The receptionist had always sent callers to the most qualified agents. She just hadn't credentialized them first. It was a costly mistake. Now it turns out the goal of instituting systems that consistently endorse or credentialize teammates is pretty easy to achieve. Here's an example from our office. My colleague, Bobette Gordon's out of office message reads as follows. Thank you for contacting me. I'm out of the office today. While I'm gone for any time sensitive issues, please contact Eileen Cartwright, email address dash dash dash. Eileen is very smart, creative, wise and resourceful. She knows everything or knows where to find it. You couldn't be in better hands. Now, if you receive that out of office message, think how much more likely you'd be to go ahead and contact Eileen about possible business and how much better you'd feel about it.
